---
title: "Cetrality metrics"
author: "Antony Bologna"
date: "2026-04-08"
output:
  word_document: default
  html_document: default
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
knitr::image_uri("img/Network_Psychometrics.jpg")
```

# 1) Centrality metrics for direct connectivity

![](Network_Psychometrics.jpg)


```{r}
# ============================================================================
# INTERACTIVE CENTRALITY CALCULATOR (Shiny + qgraph)
# ============================================================================
# This script creates an interactive web app that visualizes network centrality 
# metrics in real-time. Users can adjust edge weights via sliders and see how 
# the network structure changes instantly.
# ============================================================================

# --- 1. LOAD REQUIRED LIBRARIES ---
# Shiny: Framework for building interactive web apps in R
# qgraph: Specialized package for visualizing networks with automatic coloring
library(shiny)
library(qgraph)
## Warning: 'qgraph' was built with R version 4.5.2

# ============================================================================
# SECTION 1: USER INTERFACE (UI) DEFINITION
# ============================================================================
# Here we define the visual layout of the app: where buttons/sliders go, 
# where plots appear, etc. This is purely structural—no calculations happen here.

ui <- fluidPage(
  # Main title displayed at the top of the app
  titlePanel("Interactive Centrality Calculator (qgraph)"),
  
  # Two-column layout: sidebar (controls) on the left, main content on the right
  sidebarLayout(
    # LEFT PANEL: Contains all input controls
    sidebarPanel(
      # Instruction text for the user
      helpText("Drag the sliders to change the edge weights connected to Node X. 
               Observe how the network and metrics update in real time."),
      
      # Slider 1: Weight of edge between X and A
      # Range: -5 to 5, starting value: 1, increment by 1
      sliderInput("w_A", 
                  label = "Weight X - A:", 
                  min = -5, 
                  max = 5, 
                  value = 1, 
                  step = 1),
      
      # Slider 2: Weight of edge between X and B
      sliderInput("w_B", 
                  label = "Weight X - B:", 
                  min = -5, 
                  max = 5, 
                  value = -2, 
                  step = 1),
      
      # Slider 3: Weight of edge between X and C
      sliderInput("w_C", 
                  label = "Weight X - C:", 
                  min = -5, 
                  max = 5, 
                  value = 3, 
                  step = 1)
    ),
    
    # RIGHT PANEL: Contains the network plot and results
    mainPanel(
      # Network visualization output
      # Height is fixed at 400px for better layout control
      plotOutput("networkPlot", height = "400px"),
      
      # Horizontal dividing line for visual separation
      hr(),
      
      # Level 4 heading for the results section
      h4(textOutput("results")),
      
      # Information text explaining color conventions
      # qgraph automatically uses green/blue for positive edges and red for negative
      helpText("Note: qgraph automatically colors positive edges in green/blue 
               and negative edges in red. This makes it easy to spot inhibitory 
               relationships at a glance!")
    )
  )
)

# ============================================================================
# SECTION 2: SERVER LOGIC
# ============================================================================
# This is where all computations happen. The server listens for changes in 
# the sliders and updates the plot and text outputs reactively.

server <- function(input, output) {
  
  # ---- RENDER THE NETWORK PLOT ----
  # renderPlot() wraps the qgraph code so it runs every time an input changes
  output$networkPlot <- renderPlot({
    
    # STEP 1: Create an empty 4×4 weight matrix
    # Rows and columns represent nodes: A, B, C, X
    # All entries start at 0 (no edges)
    mat <- matrix(0, nrow = 4, ncol = 4)
    colnames(mat) <- rownames(mat) <- c("A", "B", "C", "X")
    
    # STEP 2: Fill in the matrix with slider values
    # We set both directions (X→A and A→X) because networks are typically undirected.
    # This ensures the network is symmetric.
    mat["X", "A"] <- mat["A", "X"] <- input$w_A  # Edge weight between X and A
    mat["X", "B"] <- mat["B", "X"] <- input$w_B  # Edge weight between X and B
    mat["X", "C"] <- mat["C", "X"] <- input$w_C  # Edge weight between X and C
    
    # STEP 3: Define fixed node positions
    # This prevents nodes from "dancing around" when edge weights change.
    # Layout: A in top-left, B in top-right, C in bottom-left, X in center
    # Each row represents one node (X, Y coordinates)
    posizioni <- matrix(c(-1,  1,    # Node A: x=-1, y=1
                          1,   1,    # Node B: x=1, y=1
                         -1,  -1,    # Node C: x=-1, y=-1
                          0,   0),   # Node X: x=0, y=0 (center)
                        ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)
    
    # STEP 4: Draw the network using qgraph
    # Parameters explained:
    # - layout: use the fixed positions defined above
    # - theme: "colorblind" optimizes color contrast for red/blue distinction
    # - vsize: node size (15 units)
    # - labels: show node names inside circles
    # - maximum: cap the thickness of edges at 5 (for visual stability)
    # - fade: FALSE keeps edges opaque
    # - mar: margins around the plot (5 on all sides)
    qgraph(mat,
           layout = posizioni,
           theme = "colorblind",
           vsize = 15,
           labels = TRUE,
           maximum = 5,
           fade = FALSE,
           mar = c(5, 5, 5, 5))
  })
  
  # ---- RENDER THE RESULTS TEXT ----
  # renderText() converts R output to text that displays in the UI
  output$results <- renderText({
    
    # Retrieve the current values from the three sliders
    w_A <- input$w_A
    w_B <- input$w_B
    w_C <- input$w_C
    
    # METRIC 1: Node Degree
    # Count how many edges are connected to node X (ignoring edge weight)
    # A non-zero weight = one connection
    degree <- sum(c(w_A, w_B, w_C) != 0)
    
    # METRIC 2: Node Strength
    # Sum of the absolute values of all edge weights
    # This measures the total intensity regardless of direction
    strength <- abs(w_A) + abs(w_B) + abs(w_C)
    
    # METRIC 3: Expected Influence
    # Sum of edge weights while preserving their signs
    # Positive edges increase influence; negative edges decrease it
    # This is the PRIMARY metric used in network psychometrics
    expected_influence <- w_A + w_B + w_C
    
    # Build a formatted text string combining all three metrics
    # The paste() function concatenates strings with the pipe (|) as separator
    paste("Degree:", degree,
          " | Node Strength:", strength,
          " | Expected Influence:", expected_influence)
  })
}

# ============================================================================
# SECTION 3: LAUNCH THE APPLICATION
# ============================================================================
# shinyApp() ties together the UI and server logic and starts the app
# The app will open in your browser at a local address (usually http://127.0.0.1:3838/)

shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)

# ============================================================================
# HOW TO RUN THIS SCRIPT:
# ============================================================================
# 1. Make sure you have installed both libraries:
#    install.packages("shiny")
#    install.packages("qgraph")
#
# 2. Run the entire script (Ctrl+A then Ctrl+Enter, or click "Run App" in RStudio)
#
# 3. A new window will open showing the interactive app
#
# 4. Drag the sliders and watch the network update in real time!
#
# 5. Press ESC or click the "Stop" button to close the app
# ============================================================================
```
